summary: The Informal Provider Association Coalition supports burden reduction and innovation efforts in the HTI-5 proposed rule but strongly opposes removal of key privacy, security, and C-CDA certification criteria. They emphasize maintaining robust cybersecurity protections and preserving C-CDA to ensure interoperability and protect less resourced providers. They also call for regulatory stability and thorough review before further changes.
key_arguments: Removing privacy and security certification criteria shifts costs and risks to providers, especially under-resourced ones.
Eliminating C-CDA certification risks breaking existing data exchange and forces reliance on immature FHIR standards.
Frequent regulatory changes create unpredictability and increase compliance burden for providers and developers.
Wide adoption of criteria does not guarantee continued inclusion if removed, potentially leading to additional charges.
recommendations: Preserve privacy and security certification criteria instead of removing them.
Retain C-CDA certification criteria to maintain current interoperability and protect vulnerable providers.
Avoid frequent or abrupt changes to certification and information blocking programs to ensure regulatory stability.
Collaborate with other HHS agencies to coordinate compliance requirements and provide clear implementation plans.
notable_quote: Removing these criteria opens the door for developers to charge providers for continued inclusion of these functionalities.
